skill.name: Elixir Pro
skill.description: Write idiomatic Elixir code with OTP patterns, supervision trees, and Phoenix LiveView. Masters concurrency, fault tolerance, and distributed systems. Use PROACTIVELY for Elixir refactoring, OTP design, or complex BEAM optimizations.
skill.summary: Expert Elixir developer skill for OTP, Phoenix, and BEAM optimization
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Prompt-only skill with no executable code. Provides guidance on Elixir best practices, OTP patterns, and Phoenix development. Static analysis found 0 files scanned (0 lines) with no suspicious patterns detected. This is a safe developer tool skill.

content.value_statement: Get expert-level Elixir code with OTP patterns, supervision trees, and Phoenix LiveView. The skill provides guidance on concurrency, fault tolerance, and distributed systems for production applications.
actual_capabilities: Generate idiomatic Elixir code with OTP patterns (GenServer, Supervisor, Application)
Design Phoenix applications with LiveView and clean context boundaries
Implement concurrent and fault-tolerant distributed systems
Optimize performance on the BEAM VM using profiling tools
Write ExUnit tests with doctests and property-based testing
Add Telemetry instrumentation for application observability
limitations: Cannot execute or run Elixir code
Cannot connect to databases or external services
Does not generate complete project scaffolds

use_cases.title: Build Production OTP Applications
use_cases.description: Create robust Elixir applications with proper supervision trees and fault-tolerant design patterns
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers building scalable Elixir systems

use_cases.title: Refactor to Modern Elixir
use_cases.description: Upgrade legacy Elixir code to use contemporary OTP patterns and best practices

use_cases.title: Develop Real-time Phoenix Apps
use_cases.description: Build interactive web applications with Phoenix LiveView and reactive interfaces
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developers creating real-time web features

prompt_templates.title: Basic OTP Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GenServer that manages a simple counter with increment, decrement, and get functions. Include proper initialization and termination callbacks.
prompt_templates.scenario: Learn basic OTP patterns with a straightforward stateful process

prompt_templates.title: Supervision Tree Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a supervision tree for a worker pool application. Include a Supervisor, a dynamic supervisor for workers, and a registry for tracking worker processes.

prompt_templates.title: Phoenix LiveView Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Phoenix LiveView component for real-time form validation. Include proper LiveView lifecycle handling, form state management, and error display.
prompt_templates.scenario: Build interactive Phoenix interfaces with LiveView

prompt_templates.title: Distributed System with Nodes
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a distributed counter that syncs state across multiple BEAM nodes. Use Registry for node discovery and handle node disconnection gracefully.

output_examples.input: Create a GenServer for managing a todo list
output_examples.output: A complete GenServer module with add, remove, complete, and list_todos operations. Includes state as a map, proper handle_call implementations, and example usage in the module documentation.

output_examples.input: Design a supervision strategy for a web socket handler
output_examples.output: A Supervisor configuration with one_for_one strategy, child specifications for socket supervisors, and error handling patterns for connection management.

best_practices: Prefer pattern matching over conditionals in function heads for cleaner, more expressive code
Embrace the 'let it crash' philosophy with proper supervision to handle failures gracefully
Use separate processes for isolation and concurrency instead of shared mutable state
anti_patterns: Avoid using GenServer for simple utility functions where plain module functions suffice
Do not skip supervision trees even for small applications to ensure fault tolerance
Avoid mutable state updates; use immutable data structures and functional updates

faq.question: What is OTP in Elixir?
faq.answer: OTP (Open Telecom Platform) is a set of libraries and design patterns in Elixir for building fault-tolerant, concurrent applications. It includes GenServer, Supervisor, and Application modules.

faq.question: How does supervision work in Elixir?
faq.answer: Supervision is a fault-tolerance pattern where a Supervisor process monitors child processes. When a child crashes, the Supervisor can restart it according to configured strategies.

faq.question: What is Phoenix LiveView?
faq.answer: Phoenix LiveView is a framework for building real-time web applications with Elixir. It enables server-rendered HTML with live updates over WebSockets without writing JavaScript.

faq.question: Can this skill write complete Elixir projects?
faq.answer: This skill provides guidance, code snippets, and best practices. It generates example code and architectural guidance but does not create full project scaffolds.

faq.question: How does the BEAM VM handle concurrency?
faq.answer: The BEAM VM uses lightweight processes (not OS threads) for concurrency. These processes communicate via message passing and share no mutable state, enabling massive concurrency with low overhead.

faq.question: What testing frameworks work with Elixir?
faq.answer: ExUnit is the standard testing framework. Property-based testing can be done with StreamData. Mix is used for task automation and dependency management.
